concepts.user_facing_name: internal/power — the branch your mind's code commits to; users see it only via 'versions & history'
concepts.status: internal mechanic; prior def cited dead code
concepts.technical_definition: The git branch an mngr agent's worktree commits its code to. Defaults to mngr/<agent_name>: `mngr create --branch` defaults to ':mngr/*' (_DEFAULT_NEW_BRANCH_PATTERN in cli/create.py), so an agent created with no override branches off the current branch into mngr/<agent_name>. The name is overridable via --branch BASE:NEW, and --branch BASE with no colon commits straight to BASE with no dedicated branch (created_branch_name is None). The default_branch_name()/DEFAULT_BRANCH_PREFIX helpers in primitives.py encode the same prefix but are unused dead code. In minds workspaces agents usually commit to a feature branch, not mngr/<agent_name>.
concepts.recommendation: Internal git mechanism -- don't give it its own user term; surface change history only through the 'versions & history' umbrella.

concepts.status: overloaded: 'remote host', and origin vs upstream template
concepts.working_definition: The git endpoint(s) your mind's repo syncs with. In practice there are two: your own repo on GitHub (origin) and the template repo your mind was created from (set in parent.toml). Your working branches and your runtime backups both live on origin — backups are just a separate mindsbackup/<agent-id> branch on that same remote, not a remote of their own.
concepts.technical_definition: A standard git named-URL remote (origin). Never bare 'remote' (see 'remote host').
why_hard: 'Remote' alone is ambiguous: the git remote (a VCS endpoint) vs the remote host (the compute machine that runs your mind).
Even as a git remote it covers two distinct destinations: your own repo (origin) and the upstream template repo it was cloned from (parent.toml).
Backups feel like a third destination but are just a separate branch (mindsbackup/<agent-id>) on the same origin remote — not a distinct remote.
mngr pushes pass the URL positionally; no named remote is stored in .git/config, so there isn't always a 'remote' object to point at.
to_resolve: Decide whether 'git remote' stays one infra term or splits into user-facing concepts: 'your repo' (origin) vs 'the template you came from' (the upstream it updates against).
Decide whether this should be user-facing at all — users rarely think in 'git remote' terms; they care about where their work is backed up and where template updates come from.
Capture that runtime backups live on a branch of origin, not a separate remote, so the common 'backups are a different remote' misconception is settled.

divergences.summary: mngr push passes the URL positionally; no named remote stored in .git/config.

sources.excerpt: 'git remote' for the VCS endpoint, 'remote host' for the compute machine.
